hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Are, dekho na — jab tum dhaan ki kheti mein paudhe ginte ho, toh 1, 2, 3... aise shuru karte ho na? Ye saare Natural Numbers hain. Phir agar koi kisan bolta hai ki uske paas ek bhi paudha nahi hai, matlab 0, toh 0 ko milakar ye Whole Numbers ban jaate hain. Aur agar usne fasal ke liye kisi se udhaar liya hai, toh woh negative numbers bhi use karega, jisse Integers ban jaate hain! Socho zara, kya koi aisa number hai jisko tum apni kheti mein ya gaon ki Indravati nadi mein paani maapte samay nahi dekh sakte?

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Kai baar bachche sochte hain ki — are, jiska decimal representation kabhi khatam nahi hota, matlab jo non-terminating decimal hai, woh hamesha ek irrational number hi hoga.
hi.correct: Par nahi re didi — aisa nahi hai. Jo decimal representation kabhi khatam nahi hota *aur* repeat bhi nahi hota, bas wahi irrational number hota hai. Jaise 0.333... (jise 1/3 likhte hain) toh ek rational number hai, hai na? Iska decimal khatam nahi hota par repeat hota hai.

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ek kagaz aur pencil lo. Usmein 0 aur 1 ko thoda door door mark karo. Ab, kya tum in 0 aur 1 ke beech mein kam se kam 10 alag-alag rational numbers likh sakte ho? Unhe decimal form mein bhi likhna. Socho zara, kya tum 0 aur 1 ke beech mein jitne chaaho utne numbers dhoondh sakte ho?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Hey didi, imagine tum apne gaon ke haat bazaar mein ho — you're counting the number of bhains or dhaan ki boriyan (sacks of paddy). You'd start with 1, 2, 3... right? These are Natural Numbers. If a shopkeeper runs out of everything, he has 0 items, and when you include 0, they become Whole Numbers. If someone owes money, that's like using negative numbers, which gives us Integers! Socho zara, can you think of any number you might use in your daily life in Chhattisgarh — maybe while measuring bore-basi or land for dhaan ki kheti — that doesn't fit into these categories?

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: Many times, students think that any decimal that goes on forever — never ends, a non-terminating decimal — must automatically be an irrational number.
en.correct: But that's not quite right, my dear! Only those decimals that never end *and* never repeat their pattern are truly irrational. For example, 0.333... (which is 1/3) is actually a rational number, isn't it? Its decimal goes on forever but it repeats.

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Ek kaam karo — take a piece of paper and a pencil. Draw a straight line and mark 0 and 1 a little far apart. Now, can you write down at least 10 different rational numbers that lie between 0 and 1? Try to write them in their decimal form too. Socho zara, do you think you can find as many numbers as you want between 0 and 1, didi?

hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — tumhare ghar mein jab dhaan ki kheti hoti hai ya tum bazaar mein sabzi kharidne jaate ho — agar ek kilo tamatar ka daam 'x' rupaye hai aur tum 5 kilo kharidte ho, toh kitne paise doge? 5x rupaye, hai na? Yahin se shuru hota hai polynomial ka khel: 5 ek fixed number hai (constant) aur 'x' badalta rehta hai (variable) — inko milakar hi toh expressions bante hain! Ab tum socho, agar do alag-alag sabziyan khareedo jinke daam alag-alag hon, toh total kharch ko ek expression mein kaise likhoge?

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Bahut bachche sochte hain ki har algebraic expression, jisme variables aur constants hon, ek polynomial hota hai.
hi.correct: Lekin aisa nahi hai didi! Ek algebraic expression ko 'polynomial' tabhi kehte hain jab usme variables ki power (exponent) hamesha poorn sankhya (whole number) ho. Matlab, variable na toh negative power mein ho sakta hai, na fraction mein, aur na hi square root ke andar ya denominator mein!

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — ghar mein jo bhi chhoti-chhoti cheezein milen, jaise dhaan ke kuch dane, chawal, ya kauri — unko coefficients aur variables ki power mankar alag-alag polynomials banao. Har polynomial ki degree kya hai, use pehchano aur list banao. Tumne kitne alag-alag degree ke polynomials bana paye, aur usme sabse bada coefficient kiska tha?

en.heading: Example
en.content: You know — when your family does rice farming, or when you go to the local market to buy vegetables — if one kg of tomatoes costs 'x' rupees and you buy 5 kg, how much will you pay? 5x rupees, right? This is where the game of polynomials begins: 5 is a fixed number (a constant) and 'x' keeps changing (a variable) — combining them forms expressions! Now think, if you buy two different vegetables with different prices, how would you write the total cost as an expression?

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: Many students think that any algebraic expression, as long as it has variables and constants, is a polynomial.
en.correct: But that's not quite right, my dear! An algebraic expression is called a 'polynomial' only when the power (exponent) of its variables is always a whole number. This means the variable cannot have a negative power, a fractional power, or be under a square root, or in the denominator!

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Here's an idea — use small things you find at home, like a few rice grains (dhaan ke dane), some pebbles, or even tamarind seeds, to represent coefficients and powers of variables. Create different polynomial expressions using these items. Then, identify the degree of each polynomial you've made. How many different degrees of polynomials were you able to make, and which one had the largest coefficient?
